questions.subject: Physics
questions.chapter: Center of Mass Momentum and Collision
questions.examName: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main
questions.q: A stationary body explodes into two parts of masses ' $\mathrm{M}_{1}$ ' and ${ }^{\prime} \mathrm{M}_{2}{ }^{\prime}$. They move in opposite directions with velocities ' $v_{1}$ ' and ' $v_{2}$ '. The ratio of their kinetic energies is
questions.solution:
To solve this problem, a diagram of the problem will be helpiul. Alorig with that, the corservation of linear mornentum, which is giver by. $P_{i}=p_{f} \mathrm{~ . ~ A s ~ p e r ~ N e w t a n ' s ~ s e c a n d ~ l a}$ ther montentum is conserved. The explosion occurs purely due to internal forces. Step ty step salution: Let's sart by making a diagram of the problem.
As per the problem, a stationary ball of mass (m) arnd initial velocity cercolu-0) after an explosion breaks into two masses $m_{1}$ and $m_{2}$ - These twÃ² masses move in apposite directions with velocities $v_{1}$ and $v_{2}$ respectively. We will use the conservation of linear momentum now, given by, $p_{i}=p_{f}$. The initial and linal mormenta will be equal. This means that, $p_{i}=p_{f} \Rightarrow m_{i}(0)=m_{1}\left(-v_{1}\right)+m_{2}\left(v_{2}\right) \Rightarrow m_{1}\left(v_{1}\right)=m_{2}\left(v_{2}\right)$. Hence, the ratio of the velocities will be equal to, $\frac{v_{1}}{v_{2}}=\frac{m_{2}}{m_{1}}$. The Final kinetic energy of the system after the explosion is the sum of the kinetic energies of the two bodies $E_{1}$ arnd $B_{2}$. Finding the ratio of these two kinetic entergies becomes, $\frac{E_{1}}{E_{2}}=\frac{\frac{1}{2} m_{1}\left(v_{1}\right)^{2}}{\frac{1}{2} m_{2}\left(v_{2}\right)^{2}}=\frac{m_{1}\left(v_{1}\right)^{2}}{m_{2}\left(v_{2}\right)^{2}}$. Naw, substituting in the ratio of velocites that we found out earlier, the ratio of kinetic energies becomes, $\frac{E_{1}}{E_{2}}=\left(\frac{m_{1}}{m_{2}}\right)\left(\frac{v_{1}}{v_{2}}\right)^{2}=\left(\frac{m_{1}}{m_{2}}\right)\left(\frac{m_{2}}{m_{1}}\right)^{2}=\frac{m_{2}}{m_{1}} .$ Hence it is equal to the ratio of the irnerse of the masses. Sa, the correct answer is option A. Note: Another way of solving this problem is by only using the moenemtums of the masses. From the corservation of momentum belore and after the explosion become, $p_{i}=p_{f} \Rightarrow m(0)=p_{1}+p_{2} \Rightarrow p_{1}=-p_{2}$, where $p_{1}$ and $p_{2}$ are the momencum of the masses m $_{1}$ and TR2respectively. We also know that kinetic energy is given try $E=\frac{p^{2}}{2 m}$ - Again, we will remowe the negative sign as it anly states the direclion, therefore $\frac{E_{1}}{B_{2}}=\frac{\frac{p_{1}^{2}}{2 m_{1}}}{\frac{p_{2}^{2}}{2 m_{2}}}=\frac{\frac{\left(-p_{2}\right)^{2}}{m_{1}}}{\frac{p_{2}^{2}}{m_{2}}}=\frac{\frac{p_{2}^{2}}{m_{1}}}{\frac{p_{2}^{2}}{m_{2}}}=\frac{m_{2}}{m_{1}}$
questions.source: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main

questions.subject: Physics
questions.chapter: Center of Mass Momentum and Collision
questions.examName: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main
questions.q: A body of mass $5 \mathrm{~kg}$ makes an elastic collision with another body at rest and continues to move in the original direction after collision with a velocity equal to $\frac{1}{10}$ th of its original velocity. Then the mass of the second body is
questions.solution:
Mass of the first body $m_1=5 \mathrm{~kg}$, for elastic collision $e=1$.
Suppose initially body $m_1$ moves with velocity $v$ after collision velocity becomes $\left(\frac{u}{10}\right)$. Let after collision velocity of $M$ block becomes $\left(v_2\right)$. By conservation of momentum $\begin{aligned} m_1 u_1+m_2 u_2 & =m_1 v_1+m_2 v_2 \\ \text { or } \quad 5 u+M \times 0 & =5 \times \frac{u}{10}+M v_2 \end{aligned}$
Since, $\begin{aligned} v_1-v_2 & =-e\left(u_1-u_2\right) \\ \frac{u}{10}-v_2 & =-1(u) \end{aligned}$ or $\quad \frac{u}{10}+u=v_2$
Substituting value of $v_2$ in Eq. (i) from Eq. (ii), we get $5 u=\frac{u}{2}+M\left(\frac{11 u}{10}\right)$ or $5-\frac{1}{2}=M\left(\frac{11}{10}\right)$ or $M=\frac{9 \times 10}{2 \times 11}$ or $\begin{aligned} M & =\frac{45}{11} \\ & =4.09 \mathrm{~kg} \end{aligned}$
questions.source: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main

The two bodies stick together after collision. The velocity of the composite body is

questions.q: A smooth steel ball strikes a fixed smooth steel plate at an angle θ with the vertical. If the coefficient of restitution is e, the angle at which the re-bounce will take place is
questions.solution:
Since, no force is present along the surface of plate. So, momentum conservation principle for ball is applicable along the surface of plate.



mυ sin ⁡θ1=mυ sin ⁡θ2

Or υ sin ⁡θ1=υ1 sin ⁡θ2     …(i)

e=υ1 cos ⁡θ2υ cos ⁡θ1=υ1 cos ⁡θ2υ cos ⁡θ     (θ1=θ)

∴   υ1 cos ⁡θ2=eυ cos ⁡θ     …(ii)

∴υ1 sin ⁡θ2υ1 cos ⁡θ2=υ sin ⁡θeυ cos ⁡θ=tan⁡θe

∴tan⁡θ2=tan⁡θe

∴θ2=tan-1⁡tan⁡θe

questions.subject: Physics
questions.chapter: Center of Mass Momentum and Collision
questions.examName: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main
questions.q: A projectile is launched from the origin with speed v at an angle θ from the horizon. At the highest point in the trajectory, the projectile breaks into two pieces, A and B of masses m and 2m respectively. Immediately after the breakup, piece A is at rest relative to the ground. Neglect air resistance. Which of the following sentences most accurately describes what happens next?
options: Piece <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>B</mi></math> will hit the gorund first, since it is more massive.
Both pieces have zero vertical velocity immedialtely after th breakup, and therefore they hit the ground at the same time.
Piece <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>A</mi></math> will hit the ground first, because it will have a downward velocity immediatly after the breakup.
There is no way of knowing which piece will hit the ground first, because not enough ingformation is given about th breakup.
questions.solution: At the highest point before explosion the vertical component of velocity is zero. So, as per law of conservation of momentum, after explosion also the pieces will have the same vertical component of velocity. So, in the vertical direction they will be under free fall with zero initial velocity. Hence, they will hit the ground at the same time.
questions.source: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main

questions.subject: Physics
questions.chapter: Center of Mass Momentum and Collision
questions.examName: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main
questions.q: The centre of mass of three particles of masses $1 \mathrm{~kg}, 2 \mathrm{~kg}$ and $3 \mathrm{~kg}$ is at $(2,2,2)$. The position of the fourth mass of $4 \mathrm{~kg}$ to be placed in the system as that the new centre of mass is at $(0,0,0)$ is.
questions.solution:
$m_1=1 \mathrm{~kg}, m_2=2 \mathrm{~kg}, m_3=3 \mathrm{~kg}$
Position of centre of mass $(2,2,2)$
$m_4=4 \mathrm{~kg}$
New position of centre of mass $(0,0,0)$.
For initial position,
$\begin{aligned}
& \qquad X_{\mathrm{CM}}=\frac{m_1 x_1+m_2 x_2+m_3 x_3}{m_1+m_2+m_3} \\
& \qquad 2=\frac{m_1 \times x_1+m_2 x_2+m_3 x_3}{1+2+3} \\
& m_1 x_1+m_2 x_2+m_3 x_3=12 \\
\end{aligned}$
Similarly,
$\begin{aligned}
& m_1 y_1+m_2 y_2+m_3 y_3=12 \\
& m_1 z_1+m_2 z_2+m_3 z_3=12
\end{aligned}$
and
$m_1 z_1+m_2 z_2+m_3 z_3=12$
For new position,
$\begin{gathered}
X_{\mathrm{CM}}^{\prime}=\frac{m_1 x_1+m_2 x_2+m_3 x_3+m_4 x_4}{m_1+m_2+m_3+m_4} \\
0=\frac{12+4+x_4}{1+2+3+4} \\
4 x_4=-12 \\
x_4=-3
\end{gathered}$
Similarly, $y_4=-3$
$z_4=-3$
$\therefore$ Position of fourth mass $(-3,-3,-3)$.
questions.source: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main

questions.subject: Physics
questions.chapter: Center of Mass Momentum and Collision
questions.examName: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main
questions.q: A body falls on a surface of coefficient of restitution 0.6 from a height of $1 \mathrm{~m}$. Then the body rebounds to a height of
questions.solution:
As the body falls from a height
$\begin{array}{ll} 
& \mathrm{u}=0 \\
& \mathrm{v}^2=\mathrm{u}^2+2 \mathrm{gh} \\
\therefore \quad & \mathrm{v}^2=2 \times 9.8 \times 1=19.6 \\
\therefore \quad & \mathrm{v}=\sqrt{19.6} \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}
\end{array}$
Coefficient of restitution $\mathrm{e}=\frac{\mathrm{v}}{\mathrm{u}}$
$\begin{aligned}
\mathrm{e} & =\frac{\text { Velocity after collision }\left(\mathrm{v}_{\mathrm{f}}\right)}{\text { Velocity before collision }\left(\mathrm{v}_{\mathrm{b}}\right)} \\
\therefore \quad \mathrm{v}_{\mathrm{f}} & =\mathrm{e} \times \mathrm{v}_{\mathrm{b}} \\
\mathrm{v}_{\mathrm{f}} & =0.6 \times \sqrt{19.6} \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}
\end{aligned}$
After the body rebounds,
$\begin{aligned}
& v^2=u^2-2 g h \\
& \Rightarrow u^2=2 g h
\end{aligned}$
$\therefore \quad \mathrm{h}=\mathrm{u}^2 / 2 \mathrm{~g} \text {. }$
Here, $\mathrm{u}=\mathrm{v}_{\mathrm{f}}$
$\begin{aligned}
\therefore \quad \mathrm{h} & =\frac{(0.6 \times \sqrt{19.6})^2}{2 \times 9.8} \\
& =0.36 \mathrm{~m}
\end{aligned}$
questions.source: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main

questions.subject: Physics
questions.chapter: Center of Mass Momentum and Collision
questions.examName: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main
questions.q: A ball of mass <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1</mn><mo> </mo><mi>kg</mi></math> moving along <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>X</mi></math>-direction collides elastically with a stationary ball of mass <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>m</mi></math>. The first ball (mass <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>=</mo><mn>1</mn><mo> </mo><mi>kg</mi></math>) recoils at right angle to its original direction of motion. If the second ball starts moving at an angle <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>30</mn><mo>°</mo></math> with the <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>X</mi></math>-axis, the value of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>m</mi></math>must be
questions.solution:
The given data can be diagrammatically represented as below,

According to the conservation of linear momentum,

(1) u=mv2cos30°       ...1   (∴ Along x- direction)

2 v1=mv2sin30°      ...2      (∴therefore Along y- direction)

=mv2=2 v1

On solving 1 and 2

u=3v1

Given, the collision is elastic.

∴12u2=12v12+12 mv22

⇒ 12u2=12u23+12 m23u2

⇒ 12=16+23 m

∴   m=2 kg
questions.source: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main

questions.subject: Physics
questions.chapter: Center of Mass Momentum and Collision
questions.examName: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main
questions.q: A ball is dropped from height <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">H</mi></math> on to a horizontal surface. If the coefficient of restitution is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">e</mi></math> then the total time after which it comes to rest is
questions.solution:
The time it takes for the ball to fall from a height is given by

t=2Hg

The height up to which the ball bounces after the nth collision from the floor is

Hn=e2nH

t=2Hg+22H1g+22H2g+…∞
t=2Hg1+2e+2e2+ ……..∞
t=2Hg1+e1-e
questions.source: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main

questions.subject: Physics
questions.chapter: Center of Mass Momentum and Collision
questions.examName: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main
questions.q: A bullet of mass <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>0.02</mo><mi>kg</mi></math> travelling horizontally with velocity <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>250</mn><mo> </mo><msup><mi>ms</mi><mrow><mo>−</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math> strikes a block of wood of mass <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>0.23</mo><mi>kg</mi></math> which rests on a rough horizontal surface. After the impact, the block and bullet move together and come to rest after travelling a distance of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>40</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></math>. The coefficient of sliding friction of the rough surface is (<math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">g</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>9</mn><mo>.8</mo><mo> </mo><msup><mi>ms</mi><mrow><mo>−</mo><mn>2</mn></mrow></msup></math>)
questions.solution:
After impact the bullet and block move together and come's to rest after covering a distance of 40 m.



By conservation of momentum,
m1u1+m2u2=m1v1+m2v2

or 0.02×250+0.23×0=0.0 2v+0.23 v
 
5+0=v0.25

V = 5 0 0 2 5 = 2 0 ms - 1

Now, by conservation of energy

12M⁡v⁡2=μ N.d

or 12×0.25×400=μ×0.25×9.8×40

⇒ μ=2009.8×40=0.51
questions.source: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main

questions.subject: Physics
questions.chapter: Center of Mass Momentum and Collision
questions.examName: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main
questions.q: Two particles having position vectors $\mathbf{r}_1=(3 \hat{\mathbf{i}}+5 \hat{\mathbf{j}})$ metres and $\mathbf{r}_2=(-5 \hat{\mathbf{i}}-3 \hat{\mathbf{j}})$ metres are moving with velocities $\mathbf{v}_1=(4 \hat{\mathbf{i}}+3 \hat{\mathbf{j}}) \mathrm{m} / \mathrm{s}$ and $\mathbf{v}_2=(a \hat{\mathbf{i}}+7 \hat{\mathbf{j}}) \mathrm{m} / \mathrm{s}$. If they collide after $2 \mathrm{~s}$, then the value of $a$ is :
questions.solution:
$\mathbf{r}_1=(3 \hat{\mathbf{i}}+5 \hat{\mathbf{j}}) \mathrm{m}$
$\mathbf{r}_2=(-5 \hat{\mathbf{i}}-3 \hat{\mathbf{j}}) \mathrm{m}$
$\mathbf{v}_1=(4 \hat{\mathbf{i}}+3 \hat{\mathbf{j}}) \mathrm{m} / \mathrm{s}$ and
$\mathbf{v}_2=(a \hat{\mathbf{i}}+7 \hat{\mathbf{j}}) \mathrm{m} / \mathrm{s}$
Time taken in collision, $t=\frac{r}{v}$ ...(i)
where, $r=$ resultant position vector
$=\mathbf{r}_1-\mathbf{r}_2$
$=8 \hat{\mathbf{i}}+8 \hat{\mathbf{j}}$
$v=$ resultant velocity
$\mathbf{v}_2-\mathbf{v}_1=(a-4) \hat{\mathbf{i}}+4 \hat{\mathbf{j}}$
From Eqs. (i), we get
$\therefore \quad 2=\frac{8 \hat{\mathbf{i}}+8 \hat{\mathbf{j}}}{(a-4) \hat{\mathbf{j}}+3 \hat{\mathbf{j}}} \quad[\because t=2 \mathrm{~s}]$
$(a-4) \hat{\mathbf{i}}+4 \hat{\mathbf{j}}=4 \hat{\mathbf{i}}+4 \hat{\mathbf{j}}$
$a-4=4$
$a=8$
questions.source: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main

questions.subject: Physics
questions.chapter: Center of Mass Momentum and Collision
questions.examName: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main
questions.q: The figure shows the positions and velocities of two particles. If the particles move under the mutual attraction of each other, then the position of the centre of mass at t=1 sec is x m. The value of x is
questions.solution:
According to the question, 
  
Let A be the object of mass 1 kg and moving in positive x-direction with 5 m s-1 and B be the object of mass 1 kg and moving in negative x-direction with 3 m s-1.

Now, let x1 and x2 be the positions of objects A and B after 1 sec.

We know that v = St, where, v is the velocity, S is the displacement, t is the time.

∴ x1=2 m+5 m s-1×1 sec=7 m, 

x2=8 m-3 m s-1×1 sec=5 m.

Now, the new position of the centre of mass xcm,
xcm=m1x1+m2x2m1+m2, where, m1 and m2 are the masses and x1 and x2 are the positions of respective objects. 

∴ xcm=1×7+1×51+1=6 m.
questions.source: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main

questions.subject: Physics
questions.chapter: Center of Mass Momentum and Collision
questions.examName: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main
questions.q: A wooden block of mass ' $m$ ' moves with velocity ' $v$ ' and collides with another block of mass ' $4 \mathrm{~m}$ ', which is at rest. After collision the block of mass ' $m$ ' comes to rest. The coefficient of restitution will be
questions.solution:
$$
\begin{aligned}
& \mathrm{M}_1=\mathrm{m}, \mathrm{u}_1=\mathrm{v}, \mathrm{v}_1=0 \\
& \mathrm{M}_2=4 \mathrm{~m}, \mathrm{u}_2=0, \mathrm{v}_2=?
\end{aligned}
$$
By law of conservation of momentum, we have
$$
\begin{aligned}
& \mathrm{m}_1 \mathrm{u}_1+\mathrm{m}_2 \mathrm{u}_2=\mathrm{m}_1 \mathrm{v}_1+\mathrm{m}_2 \mathrm{v}_2 \\
& \therefore \mathrm{mv}+0=0+4 \mathrm{mv}_2 \\
& \therefore \mathrm{v}=4 \mathrm{v}_2 \text { or } \mathrm{v}_2=\frac{\mathrm{v}}{4}
\end{aligned}
$$
Coefficient of restitution $\mathrm{e}=\frac{\mathrm{v}_2-\mathrm{v}_1}{\mathrm{u}_1-\mathrm{u}_2}$
$$
=\frac{\frac{\mathrm{v}}{4}-0}{\mathrm{v}-0}=\frac{1}{4}=0.25
$$
questions.source: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main

questions.subject: Physics
questions.chapter: Center of Mass Momentum and Collision
questions.examName: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main
questions.q: A particle of mass $4 m$ explodes into three pieces of masses $m, m$ and $2 \mathrm{~m}$. The equal masses move along $X$-axis and $Y$-axis with velocities $4 \mathrm{~ms}^{-1}$ and $6 \mathrm{~ms}^{-1}$ respectively. The magnitude of the velocity of the heavier mass is
questions.solution:
Let third mass particle $(2 \mathrm{~m}$ ) moves making angle $\theta$ with $X$-axis. The horizontal component of velocity of $2 \mathrm{~m}$ mass particle $=u \cos \theta$ and vertical component $=u \sin \theta$ From conservation of linear momentum in $X$-direction $\begin{array}{rlrl}\text { or } & m_1 u_1+m_2 u_2 =m_1 v_1+m_2 v_2 \\ \text { or } & 0 =m \times 4+2 m(u \cos \theta) \\ \text { or } & -4 =2 u \cos \theta\end{array}$
Again, applying law of conservation of linear momentum in $Y$-direction. $\begin{aligned} 0 & =m \times 6+2 m(u \sin \theta) \\ \Rightarrow \quad-\frac{6}{2} & =u \sin \theta \end{aligned}$ or $\quad-3=u \sin \theta \quad \ldots$ (ii) Squaring Eqs. (i) and(ii) and adding, we get $\begin{aligned} \text { (4) } +(9) & =u^2 \cos ^2 \theta+u^2 \sin ^2 \theta \\ & =u^2\left(\cos ^2 \theta+\sin ^2 \theta\right) \end{aligned}$ $\begin{aligned} \text { or } 13=u^2 \\ \text { or } u=\sqrt{13} \mathrm{~ms}^{-1}\end{aligned}$
questions.source: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main

questions.subject: Physics
questions.chapter: Center of Mass Momentum and Collision
questions.examName: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main
questions.q: A small disc of the radius <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>2</mn><mo> </mo><mi>cm</mi></math> is cut from a disc of radius <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>6</mn><mo> </mo><mi>cm</mi></math>. If the distance between their centres is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>3</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>2</mn><mo> </mo><mi>cm</mi></math>, what is the shift in the centre of mass of the disc?
questions.solution:
The situation is as below:


 

Let the radius of the complete disc is a, and that of the small disc is b. Also, let the centre of mass now shift to O2 at a distance x2 from the original centre. (Let us consider the origin to be at O.)

The position of the new centre of mass is given by:


XCM=mcxc+ m1x1mc+ m1
Where,
mc=mass of complete disc,
xc=position of the centre of the disc which is 0,
m1=mass of cut out,
x1=position of the centre of cut out.

XCM=-σπb2x1σπa2-σπb2

Here, a=6 cm, b=2 cm, x1=3.2 cm.

Hence, XCM=-σ×π22×3.2σ×π×62-σ×π×22

                         =-12.8π32π=-0.4 cm.
questions.source: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main

questions.subject: Physics
questions.chapter: Center of Mass Momentum and Collision
questions.examName: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main
questions.q: Two balls \(X(2 \mathrm{~kg})\) and \(Y(4 \mathrm{~kg})\) approach each other with equal speeds of \(10 \mathrm{~ms}^{-1}\). If the collision is perfectly elastic, then the new velocities of balls \(X\) and \(Y\) are respectively
questions.solution:
Given, \(m_1=2 \mathrm{~kg}, m_2=4 \mathrm{~kg}\)
\(v_1=v_2=10 \mathrm{~ms}^{-1}\)
In perfectly elastic collision, momentum and kinetic energy is conserved.
\(\therefore\) By conservation of momentum,
\(\begin{aligned}
& m_1 \times 10+m_2 \times(-10)=m_1 v_{1 f}+m_2 v_{2 f} \\
& \Rightarrow \quad 2 \times 10+4 \times(-10)=2 v_{1 f}+4 v_{2 f} \\
& \Rightarrow \quad-20=2\left(v_{1 f}+2 v_{2 f}\right) \\
& \Rightarrow \quad v_{1 f}+2 v_{2 f}=-10 \quad \ldots (i) \\
\end{aligned}\)
In perfectly elastic collision,
Velocity of separation \(=\) Velocity of approach
\(\begin{array}{ll}
\Rightarrow & v_{2 f}-v_{1 f}=10-(-10) \\
\Rightarrow & v_{2 f}-v_{1 f}=20 \quad \ldots (ii)
\end{array}\)
Adding Eqs. (i) and (ii), we get
\(\begin{aligned}
& 3 v_{2 f}=10 \\
& v_{2 f}=\frac{10}{3} \mathrm{~ms}^{-1}
\end{aligned}\)
From Eqs. (ii), we get
\(\begin{aligned}
& \frac{10}{3}-v_{1 f}=20 \\
& \Rightarrow \quad-v_{1 f}=20-\frac{10}{3}=\frac{50}{3} \\
& \Rightarrow \quad v_{1 f}=\frac{-50}{3} \mathrm{~ms}^{-1} \\
\end{aligned}\)
questions.source: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main

questions.subject: Physics
questions.chapter: Center of Mass Momentum and Collision
questions.examName: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main
questions.q: A block of mass $(10 \alpha) \mathrm{g}$, where $\alpha$ is a constant is moving with velocity $3 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}$ to the right collides inelastically with the block on the right with mass $10 \mathrm{~g}$ and sticks to it. The right block is connected to three springs as shown in the figure. The spring constant of each spring is $2 \mathrm{~N} / \mathrm{m}$. If the amplitude of the resulting simple harmonic motion is $\frac{1}{2 \sqrt{2}} m$, then the value of $\alpha$ is
questions.solution: Equivalent spring constant of system of springs $=\frac{3 k}{2}=3 \mathrm{~N} / \mathrm{m}$. Momentum is conserved in collision. Let blocks moves with velocity $v$ just after collision, then $$ \begin{gathered} m_1 v_1=\left(m_1+m_2\right) v \\ 10 \alpha \times 10^{-3} \times 3=(10 \alpha+10) \times 10^{-3} \times v \\ \Rightarrow \quad v=\frac{30 \alpha}{10(\alpha+1)}=\frac{3 \alpha}{\alpha+1} \mathrm{~ms}^{-1} \end{gathered} $$ Displacement of blocks is $\frac{1}{2 \sqrt{2}} \mathrm{~m}$. So, energy conservation gives, $$ \begin{aligned} & \frac{1}{2}\left(m_1+m_2\right) v^2=\frac{1}{2} k_{\text {eq }} x^2 \text { [friction is absent] } \\ & \Rightarrow \quad \frac{(10 \alpha+10) \times 10^{-3} \times 9 \alpha^2}{(\alpha+1)^2}=3 \times \frac{1}{8} \\ & \Rightarrow \quad \frac{9 \alpha^2}{100(\alpha+1)}=\frac{3}{8} \\ & 24 \alpha^2=100 \alpha+100 \Rightarrow \alpha=5 \end{aligned} $$
questions.source: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main

questions.subject: Physics
questions.chapter: Center of Mass Momentum and Collision
questions.examName: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main
questions.q: A mass of $2.9 \mathrm{~kg}$ is suspended from a string of length $50 \mathrm{~cm}$ and is at rest. Another body of mass $100 \mathrm{~g}$, which is moving horizontally with a velocity of $150 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}$ strikes and sticks to it. Subsequently when the string makes an angle of $60^{\circ}$ with the vertical, the tension in the string is $\left(g=10 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}^2\right)$
questions.solution:
From law of conservation of momentum, we known,
$$ \begin{array}{lc} & m_1 u_1+m_2 u_2=m_1 v_1+m_2 v_2 \\ u_1=0, u_2=150 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}, m_1=2.9 \mathrm{~kg} \text { and } m_2=0.1 \mathrm{~kg} \\ \text { So, } & 2.9 \times 150=(2.9+0.1) \mathrm{v} \\ \Rightarrow & \frac{2.9 \times 150}{3}=v \\ \Rightarrow & \\ \text { Also, } & T \sin \theta=\frac{m v^2}{r} \end{array} $$ Putting the values and solving, we get $T=135 \mathrm{~N}$.
questions.source: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main

questions.subject: Physics
questions.chapter: Center of Mass Momentum and Collision
questions.examName: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main
questions.q: Three uniform thin aluminium rods each of length $2 \mathrm{~m}$ form an equilateral triangle $P Q R$ as shown in the figure. The mid point of the $\operatorname{rod} P Q$ is at the origin of the coordinate system. If the temperature of the system of rods increases by $50^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$, the increase in $y$-coordinate of the centre of mass of the system of the rods is ............ $\mathrm{mm}$. (Coefficient of volume expansion of aluminium $=12 \sqrt{3} \times 10^{-6} \mathrm{~K}^{-1}$ )
questions.solution: Initially, $$ \begin{aligned} & \mathbf{r}_{\mathrm{COM}}=\frac{m_1 \boldsymbol{r}_1+m_2 \boldsymbol{r}_2+m_3 \boldsymbol{r}_3}{\left(m_1+m_2+m_3\right)} \\ & \Rightarrow \mathbf{r}_{\mathrm{COM}}=\frac{1}{3}\left(\frac{\sqrt{3}}{2} \times 1 \times 2\right) \hat{\mathbf{j}} \Rightarrow \mathrm{r}_{\mathrm{COM}}=\frac{1}{\sqrt{3}} \hat{\mathbf{j}} \end{aligned} $$ Final length, $$ l_f=l_i(1+\alpha \Delta T) $$ or, $l_f=l_i\left(1+\frac{\gamma}{3} \Delta T\right) \quad[\because \gamma=3 \alpha]$ or, $l_f=2\left(1+\frac{12 \sqrt{3} \times 10^{-6}}{3} \times 50\right)$ or, $l_f=2\left(1+4 \times 50 \times \sqrt{3} \times 10^{-6}\right)$ or, $l_f=2\left(1+2 \sqrt{3} \times 10^{-4}\right)$ $$ \begin{aligned} & \text { So, } \quad\left(\mathbf{r}_{\text {COM }}\right)_{\text {final }}=\frac{1}{3} \times \frac{\sqrt{3}}{2} \times\left(1+2 \sqrt{3} \times 10^{-4}\right) \times 2 \hat{\mathbf{j}} \\ & =\left(\frac{1}{\sqrt{3}}+2 \times 10^{-4}\right) \hat{\mathbf{j}} \end{aligned} $$ So, $\quad \Delta y=\left(\mathrm{r}_{\text {СOM }}\right)_{\text {final }}-\left(\mathrm{r}_{\text {СоM }}\right)_{\text {initial }}$ $=2 \times 10^{-4} \mathrm{~m}=0.2 \mathrm{~mm}$
questions.source: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main

questions.subject: Physics
questions.chapter: Center of Mass Momentum and Collision
questions.examName: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main
questions.q: A ball falls from a height $h$ and rebounds after striking the floor. The coefficient of restitution is $e$. The maximum distance covered before it comes to rest is
questions.solution: Ball falls from height $h$ then formula for height covered by it in $n$th rebound is given by $h_n=h e^{2 n}$ where $e=$ coefficient of restitution $n=$ number of rebound Total distance travelled by particle before rebounding has stopped. $H=h+2 h_1+2 h_2+2 h_n+\ldots$ $H=h+2 h e^2+2 h e^4+2 h e^6+2 h e^8+\ldots$ $=h+2 h\left(e^2+e^4+e^6+e^8+\ldots\right)$ $=h+2 h\left[\frac{e^2}{1-e^2}\right]$ $=h\left[1+\frac{2 e^2}{1-e^2}\right]$ $=h\left[\frac{1+e^2}{1-e^2}\right]$
questions.source: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main

questions.subject: Physics
questions.chapter: Center of Mass Momentum and Collision
questions.examName: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main
questions.q: A rocket motor consumes $100 \mathrm{~kg}$ of fuel per second exhausting it with a speed of $5 \mathrm{~km} / \mathrm{s}$. The speed of the rocket when its mass is reduced to $\frac{1^{\text {th }}}{20}$ of its initial mass, is (Assume initial speed to be zero and ignored gravitational and viscous forces.)
questions.solution:
Key Idea Velocity of a rocket at any timet, $v=u\left(\frac{m_0}{m}\right)-g t$
where, $u=$ speed of exhausted gases,
$m_0=$ initial mass of the rocket
and $m=$ mass of the rocket at time $t$
Given, fuel burned rate, $\left(\frac{d m}{d t}\right)=100 \mathrm{~kg} / \mathrm{s}, u=5$
$\mathrm{km} / \mathrm{s}$
Then, $v=5 \ln \left(\frac{m_0}{m}\right)-g t$

As, it is given that the gravitational force is ignored and mass of rocket is reduced to $\frac{1}{20}$ th of it's initial mass i.e, $m=\frac{1}{20} m_0$.
or
$$
\frac{m_0}{m}=20
$$
So, $v=5 \ln (20) \mathrm{km} / \mathrm{s}$
Hence, the correct option is (3).
questions.source: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main

questions.subject: Physics
questions.chapter: Center of Mass Momentum and Collision
questions.examName: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main
questions.q: Look at the drawing given in the figure which has been drawn with ink of uniform line-thickness. The mass of ink used to draw each of the two inner circles, and each of the two line segments is m. The mass of the ink used to draw the outer circle is 6m. The coordinates of the centres of the different parts are: outer circle (0, 0) left inner circle (−a, a), right inner circle (a, a), vertical line (0, 0) and horizontal line (0, −a). The y-coordinate of the centre of mass of the ink in this drawing is
questions.solution:
y CM = m 1 y 1 + m 2 y 2 + m 3 y 3 + m 4 y 4 + m 5 y 5 m 1 + m 2 m 3 + m 4 + m 5

        = 6 m 0 + m a + m a + m 0 + m - a 6 m + m + m + m + m

        = a 1 0
questions.source: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main

questions.subject: Physics
questions.chapter: Center of Mass Momentum and Collision
questions.examName: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main
questions.q: A particle <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">A</mi></math> suffers an oblique elastic collision with a particle <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">B</mi></math> which is initially at rest. If their masses are same, then after the collision
options: they will move in opposite directions

<math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">A</mi></math> continues to move in the original direction while <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">B</mi></math> remains at rest
they will move in mutually perpendicular directions

<math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">A</mi></math> comes to rest and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">B</mi></math> starts moving in the direction of the original motion of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">A</mi></math>
questions.solution:
as collision is elastic

12mu2=12mv12+12v22

u2=v12+v22 .........(1)

Momentum is conserved

mu→=mv→1+mv→2

u→=v→1+v→2

u2=v12+v22+2v1.v2cos⁡θ .......(2)

Solving (1) and (2)

2v1.v2cos⁡θ=0

cos⁡θ=0

θ=90o

i.e. v1 & v2 are mutually perpendicular.
questions.source: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main

questions.subject: Physics
questions.chapter: Center of Mass Momentum and Collision
questions.examName: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main
questions.q: In the figure shown a semicircular area is removed from a uniform square plate of side l and mass (before removing) m. The x-coordinate of centre of mass of remaining portion is (The origin is at the centre of square)
questions.solution:
The centre of mass about x-axis is given as,

xcm=m1x1+m2x2 m1+m2

Here, m1= mass of the square plate=m

x1=centre of mass of the square plate =0 (As the origin is at centre of the square)
m2= mass of the removed part=mass density of square×area of the semicircular=-ml2πl222=-π8m

x2=centre of mass of the removed part=l2-43πl2=l21-43π

Now, the centre of mass of the system will be,xcm=-πm8×l21-43πm-π8m

Hence, the centre of mass of remaining portion xcm=-lπ-432(8-π).
questions.source: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main

questions.subject: Physics
questions.chapter: Center of Mass Momentum and Collision
questions.examName: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main
questions.q:
A bouncing ball of mass $200 \mathrm{~g}$ falls from the height of $5 \mathrm{~m}$ on a horizontal ground. After every impact with the ground, the velocity of the ball decreases by $\frac{1}{2}$ times.
The total momentum, the ball imparts on to the ground after 3 impacts is $\left(\right.$ Let $\left.g=10 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}^2\right)$
questions.solution:
Given, mass of ball, $m=200 \mathrm{~g}=0.2 \mathrm{~kg}$
and height of the ball, $h=5 \mathrm{~m}$
$\therefore$ Velocity of ball at the time of first impact,
$v_1^{\prime}=\frac{v_1}{2}=\frac{10}{2}=5 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}$
$\therefore$ Momentum imparted by the ball in first impact,
$\begin{aligned} p_1 & =m v_1-m\left(-v_1^{\prime}\right) \\ & =m v_1+m v_1^{\prime}\end{aligned}$
$\begin{aligned} & =0.2 \times 10+0.2 \times 5 \\ p_1 & =3 \mathrm{~kg} \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}\end{aligned}$
Similarly, velocity of the ball at the time of second impact and its recoil velocity will be $5 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}$ and $\frac{5}{2} \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}$.
Hence, momentum imparted by ball in second impact,
$\begin{aligned} p_2 & =m \times 5-m\left(-\frac{5}{2}\right) \\ & =0.2 \times 5+0.2 \times \frac{5}{2}=1.5=\frac{3}{2} \mathrm{~kg} \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}\end{aligned}$
Again, velocity of ball at the time of third impact and its recoil velocity will be $\frac{5}{2} \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}$ and $\frac{5}{4} \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}$.
Hence, momentum imparted by ball in third impact,
and its recoil velocity will be $\frac{5}{2} \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}$ and $\frac{5}{4} \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}$.
Hence, momentum imparted by ball in third impact,
$\begin{aligned} & p_3=m\left(\frac{5}{2}\right)-m\left(-\frac{5}{4}\right) \\ & p_3=0.2 \times \frac{5}{2}+0.2 \times \frac{5}{4}=\frac{3}{4} \mathrm{~kg}-\mathrm{m} / \mathrm{s}\end{aligned}$
$\therefore$ Total momentum imparted by the ball to the ground,
$\begin{aligned} p & =p_1+p_2+p_3 \\ & =3+\frac{3}{2}+\frac{3}{4}=\frac{21}{4} \mathrm{~kg} \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}\end{aligned}$
questions.source: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main

questions.subject: Physics
questions.chapter: Center of Mass Momentum and Collision
questions.examName: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main
questions.q: A uniform rectangular thin sheet ABCD of mass M has length a and breadth b, as shown in the figure. If the shaded portion HBGO is cut-off, the coordinates of the centre of mass of the remaining portion will be:
questions.solution:
If the total mass of object mass M then the mass removed part will be M4 .

XCM=M·a2-M43a4M-M4=Ma2-3Ma163M4=512a
YCM=M·b2-M43b4M-M4=Mb2-3Mb163M4=512b
questions.source: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main

questions.subject: Physics
questions.chapter: Center of Mass Momentum and Collision
questions.examName: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main
questions.q: A system consists of three particles each of mass ' $\mathrm{m}_1$ ' placed at the corners of an equilateral triangle of side ' $\frac{\mathrm{L}}{3}$, A particle of mass ' $\mathrm{m}_2$ ' is placed at the mid point of any one side of the triangle. Due to the system of particles, the force acting on $\mathrm{m}_2$ is
questions.solution:
From the fig., we can see that the forces due to masses at Q and R cancel each other as they are equal and opposite. The force at $\mathrm{P}$ is only due to $\mathrm{m}_1$. In $\triangle P Q S$, $\mathrm{h}=\frac{\mathrm{L}}{3} \cos 30^{\circ}=\frac{\mathrm{L} \sqrt{3}}{6}$ $\therefore \quad$ Force on $\mathrm{m}_2$ due to $\mathrm{m}_1$ at $\mathrm{P}$ is
$\begin{aligned} F & =\frac{G m_1 m_2}{\left(\frac{L \sqrt{3}}{6}\right)^2} \\ & =\frac{G m_1 m_2 \cdot 12}{L^2} \\ & =\frac{12 \mathrm{Gm}_1 \mathrm{~m}_2}{\mathrm{~L}^2}\end{aligned}$
questions.source: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main

questions.subject: Physics
questions.chapter: Center of Mass Momentum and Collision
questions.examName: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main
questions.q: A bullet of mass <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>m</mi></math> moving horizontally with speed <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>v</mi><mn>0</mn></msub></math> hits a wooden block of mass <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>M</mi></math> that is suspended from a massless string. The bullet gets lodged into the block and comes into halt. If the block-bullet combine swings to a maximum height <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>h</mi></math> then how much of the initial kinetic energy of the bullet is lost in the collision?
questions.solution:
Given: Mass of bullet m, the velocity of bullet v0 and mass of the wooden block M.

Let the velocity of the bullet after the collision with the wooden block be v

Applying the law of conservation of momentum, we obtain

mv0=M+mv

v=mv0M+m

Now applying the law of conservation of mechanical energy, we obtain

12mv02=mgh

Putting the value of v in the above equation, we get  

12M+mmv0M+m2=M+mgh

Maximum height of the block-bullet system h=12mv02Mm+M
questions.source: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main

questions.subject: Physics
questions.chapter: Center of Mass Momentum and Collision
questions.examName: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main
questions.q: Three bodies each of mass $1 \mathrm{~kg}$ are situated at the vertices of an equilateral triangle of side $1 \mathrm{~m}$. The $x y$-coordinates of centre of mass of the system are.
questions.solution: $\begin{aligned} & X_{\mathrm{cm}}=\frac{0+(1 \times 1)+(1 \times 0.5)}{3}=\frac{1}{2} \mathrm{~m} \\ & Y_{\mathrm{cm}}=\frac{0+0+\left(1 \times \frac{\sqrt{3}}{2}\right)}{3}=\frac{1}{2 \sqrt{3}} \mathrm{~m}\end{aligned}$
questions.source: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main

questions.subject: Physics
questions.chapter: Center of Mass Momentum and Collision
questions.examName: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main
questions.q: A bullet is fired from the gun. It hits the solid block resting on a frictionless surface, gets embedded into it and both move jointly. In this process,
options: Both momentum and kinetic energy are not conserved.
Bot the momentum and kinetic energy are conserved.
questions.solution:
Concept based.
This is a case of a perfectly inelastic collision in which linear momentum is conserved but kinetic energy is not conserved.
questions.source: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main
